content: CARDIAC SURGERY - POST-OPERATIVE CARE GUIDELINES. Cardiac surgery, including coronary artery bypass grafting (CABG), valve replacement, and other procedures, requires comprehensive post-operative care focused on wound healing, cardiac rehabilitation, medication management, and lifestyle modification. POST-SURGICAL DIET. Heart bypass surgery recovery diet recommendations. Immediate Post-Operative (Hospital Days 1-5): Clear liquids progressing to regular diet as tolerated. Small, frequent meals (6 small meals vs. 3 large). Low sodium to manage fluid retention (less than 2000mg daily). Early Recovery Phase (Weeks 1-4): Continue low-sodium diet (1500-2000mg daily). Fluid restriction may be necessary (typically 1.5-2 liters daily). High protein for wound healing (lean meats, fish, eggs, legumes). Limit saturated fat and trans fats. Cardiac Rehabilitation Diet (Long-term): Mediterranean-style eating pattern strongly recommended. Fruits and vegetables: 5-9 servings daily. Whole grains: Replace refined carbohydrates. Lean proteins: Fish (especially fatty fish 2x weekly), poultry, legumes, limited red meat. Healthy fats: Olive oil, nuts, avocados instead of butter and lard. Coumadin (Warfarin) Patients: Maintain consistent vitamin K intake (leafy greens). Avoid large fluctuations in green vegetable consumption. Regular monitoring of INR levels. Foods to Avoid/Minimize: High-sodium foods: canned soups, processed meats, pickles, restaurant meals. Saturated fats: fatty cuts of meat, full-fat dairy, butter. Trans fats: fried foods, commercially baked goods. Excessive caffeine: may trigger arrhythmias in some patients. EXERCISE AND ACTIVITY PROGRESSION. When can I resume exercise after heart surgery? Phase 1: Immediate Recovery (Weeks 1-2): Walking only: Start with 5-10 minutes, 2-3 times daily. Pace should allow conversation without breathlessness. Stop if experiencing chest pain, severe shortness of breath, or dizziness. Avoid lifting anything heavier than 5-10 pounds. Phase 2: Early Rehabilitation (Weeks 3-6): Increase walking duration to 15-20 minutes, 2 times daily. Add gentle stretching (avoid chest wall strain). Stair climbing as tolerated (no carrying items while climbing). Phase 3: Structured Cardiac Rehabilitation (Weeks 6-12): Formal cardiac rehab program enrollment (strongly recommended). Supervised exercise with cardiac monitoring. Progressive increase in intensity and duration. Phase 4: Independent Exercise (3+ months): Continue aerobic exercise: walking, cycling, swimming (once sternum healed). Target: 30 minutes moderate-intensity activity, 5 days per week. Strength training may be added at 8-12 weeks with surgeon clearance. Lifting Restrictions: Sternum healing requires 6-12 weeks. No lifting, pushing, or pulling over 5-10 pounds for 6-8 weeks. No overhead reaching with heavy items. When lifting, keep elbows close to body, lift with legs. Return to Work: Desk work: 6-8 weeks typically. Physical labor: 12+ weeks, may require restrictions. INFECTION RECOGNITION. Signs of heart infection after cardiac surgery. Sternal Wound Infection Signs: Increased redness, warmth, or swelling along chest incision. Purulent (pus) drainage from incision. Incision separation or clicking/popping sensation in sternum. Fever greater than 100.4°F (38°C). Increased pain at surgical site after initial improvement. Endocarditis (Heart Valve Infection) Warning Signs: Persistent fever without obvious source. Night sweats, fatigue and weakness out of proportion to expected recovery. New or changing heart murmur, shortness of breath. Unexplained weight loss, joint pain or muscle aches. Immediate Action Required: Any fever over 100.4°F within 6 weeks of surgery requires immediate evaluation. Wound drainage or separation needs prompt surgical assessment. Prevention Strategies: Complete all prescribed antibiotics. Strict sterile technique for wound care. Avoid submerging incision in water until fully healed (typically 4-6 weeks). Maintain excellent oral hygiene (dental infections can seed heart valves). Prophylactic antibiotics required for dental work for 6 months post-valve surgery. BLOOD THINNER MANAGEMENT. Blood thinner management after heart surgery. Types of Blood Thinners Post-Cardiac Surgery: Aspirin: Most patients prescribed 81-325mg daily lifelong. Essential for graft patency in bypass patients. Also antiplatelet effect for valve patients. Warfarin (Coumadin): Required for mechanical heart valves (lifelong). May be used for tissue valves temporarily (3-6 months). Requires regular INR monitoring (target typically 2.0-3.0). Direct Oral Anticoagulants (DOACs): Apixaban (Eliquis), Rivaroxaban (Xarelto), Dabigatran (Pradaxa). Increasingly used post-cardiac surgery. No routine monitoring required. Critical Safety Information: Bleeding Precautions: Use soft toothbrush, electric razor. Avoid activities with high trauma risk. Report any bleeding that doesn't stop with 10-15 minutes pressure. Seek immediate care for: head injury (even minor), blood in urine/stool, vomiting blood. Monitoring Requirements: Warfarin: INR testing frequency varies (weekly initially, then monthly when stable). Keep consistent vitamin K intake if on warfarin. Drug Interactions: Many medications affect anticoagulation. Always inform ALL healthcare providers about blood thinner use. Check before starting new medications (including OTC and supplements).
